title: French and Dutch colonization
description: Practice your skills as you answer questions about early colonization in the Americas.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did French and Dutch colonies in North America stay close to rivers like the St. Lawrence and the Hudson?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about how Europeans moved goods in the 1600s, before railroads or highways existed.

choices.content: Rivers provided key trade routes for moving furs and fish to European markets.
Rivers gave access to large gold mines hidden deep in the interior of North America.
Rivers were safe zones that were far from Spanish military power.
Rivers allowed missionaries to travel more easily and convert Native nations.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did French and Dutch colonies differ from Spanish colonies in their treatment of Native peoples?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about the economic base of French and Dutch colonies—what did they need Native peoples’ help with?

choices.content: French and Dutch colonies built alliances and trade partnerships, rather than a system of forced labor.
French and Dutch colonies avoided contact with Native communities, choosing instead to focus on trading fur.
French and Dutch colonies conquered large populations, rather than forming alliances and making treaties.
They sent large numbers of settlers to create farming towns, rather than building small colonies.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did Native nations benefit from forming alliances with European powers in North America in the 1600s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about what Native leaders hoped to gain by allying with Europeans.

choices.content: They gained trade goods and military support to challenge rival groups.
They secured European promises that guaranteed lasting protection of their lands.
They could control European settlements by setting firm territorial boundaries.
They promoted shared religion and culture to end long-standing rivalries.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did French and Dutch colonies attract fewer European settlers than Spanish colonies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about the difference between colonies built for farming and those built for trading.

choices.content: They focused on trade in furs and fish rather than on large farming empires.
They lacked interest in spreading Christianity to Native peoples.
They struggled with harsh climates that made farming nearly impossible.
They discouraged families from migrating to their colonies.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the main purpose of New Amsterdam in the Dutch colonies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about why the Dutch valued Manhattan’s location at the mouth of the Hudson River.

choices.content: To serve as a trading center for fur and goods in the Atlantic world
To develop large plantations that produced sugar for export
To provide a safe place for Protestants in search of religious freedom
To conquer Spanish colonies and take control of all American resources

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did the economy of New France focus on trade instead of large-scale farming?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about what Europeans most wanted from North America and how Native nations helped the French meet that demand.

choices.content: European demand for furs made trading with Native nations more profitable than raising crops.
The cold climate and rocky soil made it impossible to grow any crops in New France.
Most French colonists preferred to hunt and fish instead of living in farming communities.
Trade was safer and easier than farming because it didn’t depend on weather or seasons.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What role did Samuel de Champlain play in strengthening New France?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He founded Quebec City and built alliances with Native neighbors.
He discovered rich silver mines and exported them to France.
He converted most of the Iroquois to Catholicism through peaceful missions.
He banned all trade with Native peoples to keep profits in French hands.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How was New Amsterdam different from other European colonies in North America during the 1600s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about what made New Amsterdam a thriving trading city and how that shaped the colony’s identity.

choices.content: It developed into a diverse city with settlers from many backgrounds.
It was established as a safe place for Puritans to practice their religion.
It was built around large plantations that grew crops to export to Europe.
It was founded as a military stronghold to protect Dutch colonies in the Americas.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the defeat of the Spanish Armada in 1588 impact European colonization in North America?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about which country lost strength in 1588, and which rivals gained more freedom at sea.

choices.content: It weakened Spain’s naval power, creating opportunities for England and the Netherlands.
It forced Spain to give all of its colonies in Central and South America to England and France.
It gave France complete control over the fur trade in the Great Lakes region.
It ended religious rivalry between Catholics and Protestants in Europe.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did European religious conflict shape colonization in the Americas?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Consider the two major religious groups in Europe that may have carried over to competition in the Americas.

choices.content: It drove Catholic and Protestant nations to compete with each other for colonies.
It led most European nations to slow their colonization efforts for religious and moral reasons.
It encouraged Spain and England to join together against Protestant France.
It led to peace agreements that kept religious divisions out of colonization efforts.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did Native American groups mainly interact with French and Dutch colonists in North America?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about how Native groups used Europeans to pursue their own political and military goals.

choices.content: They formed alliances to gain trade goods and strengthen themselves against rivals.
They avoided contact and tried to stay neutral in colonial rivalries.
They were forced into service in the French and Dutch colonial armies.
They set aside their own rivalries to resist colonization efforts by French and Dutch settlers.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In what way did Native nations influence competition between the French and the Dutch in North America?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about how Native leaders could use competition between European traders to their own advantage.

choices.content: They shifted trading partnerships to gain better deals and military support.
They accepted European control in exchange for fixed prices on trade goods.
They supplied both sides equally to keep the peace instead of gaining advantages.
They built a single confederation that controlled all trade between Europeans and Native peoples.
